<content>1: ---
2: name: ai-native-repo-bootstrap

Use when a repo needs baseline docs, quality gates, CI and release scaffolding, ruleset-as-code, or an audit and remediation plan against that baseline."
4: ---
5: 
6: # AI-Native Repo Bootstrap
7: 
8: ## Overview
9: 
10: Use this skill to bring a repository to an AI-native operating baseline so humans and AI agents can work safely, quickly, and with enough context to make good decisions.
11: 
12: This skill is not a generic template copier. It classifies repository maturity, audits local and remote state, applies or proposes the right baseline, preserves intentional maturity-based exceptions, and verifies the result.
13: 
14: Prefer judgment over box-checking. File presence alone is not enough; audit whether the repository is truthful about its current state, whether commands are actually runnable, and whether any template residue or placeholder content still misleads users or agents.
15: 
16: When GitHub governance is in scope, keep three states separate in both reasoning and reporting:
17: 
18: - local desired state stored in repo files
19: - live remote state currently configured on GitHub
20: - default-branch activation state for workflows and protections that may lag local branch changes
21: 
22: ## Use This Skill When
23: 
24: - A user wants to bootstrap a new repository for AI-native development.
25: - A repository needs stronger docs, guardrails, and feedback loops before AI agents can work effectively.
26: - A repository should be aligned with a modern baseline for `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `pre-commit`, CI, release automation, and GitHub rulesets.
27: - A docs-first repository needs an honest baseline without pretending application code or tests already exist.
28: - A repository needs an audit and remediation plan for local files plus remote GitHub governance.
29: 
30: ## Do Not Use This Skill For
31: 
32: - Product feature implementation unrelated to repository bootstrap.
33: - One-off code or CI fixes with no repository-baseline intent.
34: - Secret provisioning or credential management.
35: - Organization-wide GitHub administration outside the target repository.
36: - Destructive git cleanup or bypassing hooks.
37: 
38: ## Core Workflow
39: 
40: 1. Classify repository maturity and decide early whether the template-repo lens applies.
41: 2. Audit the repository against the baseline rubric in `references/baseline-rubric.md`.
42: 3. Choose the right workflow: `new_repo` or `upgrade_repo`.
43: 4. Check for current-state truthfulness, placeholder types, and stale template residue across `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, contributor docs, and workflow comments.
44: 5. Separate minimum acceptable baseline findings from stronger target-state improvements.
45: 6. Apply or propose local baseline changes first.
46: 7. Audit remote GitHub governance separately using `references/github-governance.md`.
47: 8. Keep local desired state, live remote state, and default-branch activation state distinct in the report.
48: 9. Require explicit user approval before any remote `gh api` apply phase.
49: 10. Re-verify local and remote state and report residual gaps.
50: 
51: ## Workflow: `new_repo`
52: 
53: Use this workflow when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still close to raw template state.
54: 
55: ### Goals
56: 
57: - Establish the required baseline artifacts.
58: - Replace placeholders or explicitly document what remains intentionally deferred.
59: - Set up local quality loops and CI scaffolding that match current maturity.
60: - Prepare remote governance as auditable desired state.
61: 
62: ### Actions
63: 
64: 1. Confirm repo identity, purpose, and current maturity.
65: 2. Decide whether the repo is a normal docs-first repo or a template repo whose product is scaffolding itself.
66: 3. Add or update baseline repo docs and governance files.
67: 4. Add tech-agnostic local guardrails first.
68: 5. Add CI and release scaffolding that are honest about the current stage of the repo.
69: 6. Prepare ruleset-as-code and remote governance guidance.
70: 7. If GitHub is in scope, document repo-settings desired state in addition to ruleset intent.
71: 8. Run validation and produce a readiness report.
72: 
73: ## Workflow: `upgrade_repo`
74: 
75: Use this workflow when the repository already has meaningful structure and needs to be brought into baseline alignment.
76: 
77: ### Goals
78: 
79: - Preserve valid repo-specific conventions.
80: - Identify missing or misaligned baseline elements.
81: - Respect intentional maturity-based exceptions.
82: - Produce an ordered remediation plan and verification report.
83: 
84: ### Actions
85: 
86: 1. Inventory the current local and remote state.
87: 2. Compare the repository to the baseline rubric.
88: 3. Classify placeholders as downstream-customization, maturity-based, stale residue, or governance-invalidating.
89: 4. Check command-to-filesystem consistency so current-state docs do not claim tooling that is not provisioned.
90: 5. Gather evidence for command realism from files such as manifests, lockfiles, scripts, workflow definitions, and tool configs, and apply the same standard to `AGENTS.md` as to `README.md`.
91: 6. Verify CODEOWNERS entries are concrete and plausibly valid for GitHub resolution, not just present.
92: 7. Treat direct remote apply examples and hook-bypass instructions as explicit safety findings when they are not fenced by audit-first or emergency-only guidance.
93: 8. Classify gaps by severity and by local versus remote scope.
94: 9. Apply or propose local fixes with minimal churn.
95: 10. Audit remote governance and ask for approval before any apply phase.
96: 11. Re-run validation and document residual risks.
97: 
98: ## Maturity Profiles
99: 
100: Always map the repository to a maturity profile using `references/maturity-profiles.md`.
101: 
102: - `docs-first`: planning and governance are present; implementation is absent or minimal.
103: - `early-implementation`: code exists but quality gates and automation are still forming.
104: - `active-delivery`: code, tests, release expectations, and team workflows are established.
105: 
106: Recommendations must be adjusted to the profile. Do not force active-delivery expectations onto a docs-first repository.
107: 
108: Do not let sophisticated ruleset or release automation inflate maturity by itself. A repository can remain `docs-first` even when governance and release scaffolding are advanced.
109: 
110: Template repositories need extra care. A repo whose product is reusable scaffolding may be docs-first in implementation but strong in repo operations. Evaluate whether placeholders are appropriate for downstream consumers or misleading for the current repo.
111: 
112: ## Local Baseline Expectations
113: 
114: Use `references/baseline-rubric.md` as the canonical definition of good.
115: 
116: At minimum, evaluate:
117: 
118: - repo intent and documentation clarity
119: - current-state truthfulness and command realism
120: - governance and contribution standards
121: - local feedback loops
122: - CI quality enforcement
123: - release scaffolding
124: - remote policy as code

128: - maturity-based exceptions

130: Treat governance-invalidating placeholders as higher severity than ordinary template text. Broken ownership, broken support links, and unsafe direct-mutation guidance should not be scored like harmless downstream customization markers.
131: 
132: Treat documentation or agent instructions that jump directly to remote mutation without an audit-first or approval-gated framing as an explicit safety smell.
133: 
134: Treat hook-bypass guidance such as `--no-verify` as a real safety finding unless it is narrowly framed as a human-only emergency exception.
135: 
136: ## Remote Governance Rules
137: 
138: Treat remote GitHub operations as a separate, approval-gated phase.
139: 
140: - Audit first.
141: - Show the delta clearly.
142: - Require explicit approval before create, update, or sync operations.
143: - Verify live parity after any remote apply step.
144: 
145: Use `references/github-governance.md` for repo settings, ruleset audit, ruleset sync, and parity verification patterns.
146: 
147: Treat GitHub-specific ruleset sync workflows, drift detection, and release identity integrations as stronger target-state patterns when GitHub is in scope, not as universal baseline requirements.
148: 
149: ## Output Standard
150: 
151: Always produce a clear report that includes:
152: 
153: 1. repository maturity classification
154: 2. compliant baseline items
155: 3. missing or misaligned items
156: 4. current-state truthfulness or command-realism issues
157: 5. stale template residue or placeholder classification findings

162: 10. default-branch activation summary
163: 11. local remediation plan or applied changes
164: 12. remote remediation plan or applied changes
165: 13. validation results
166: 14. residual risks and follow-ups
167: 
168: Use these package resources to keep outputs consistent:
169: 
170: - `templates/delta-report-template.md`
171: - `templates/check-results-template.md`
172: - `templates/execution-log-template.json.md`
173: - `checklists/preflight-checklist.md`
174: - `checklists/verification-checklist.md`
175: 
176: ## Safety Rules
177: 
178: - Never apply destructive operations without explicit user request.
179: - Never silently mutate remote GitHub settings or rulesets.
180: - Never claim a repository is ready without running the relevant checks.
181: - Never treat GitHub-specific guidance as universal when the neutral core is sufficient.
182: - Clearly label assumptions, deferred items, and unresolved questions.

name: ai-native-repo-bootstrap
description: "Bootstrap or upgrade a repository for AI-native development with clear repo context, modern devex guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance. Use when a repo needs baseline docs, quality gates, CI and release scaffolding, ruleset-as-code, or an audit and remediation plan against that baseline."
---

# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap

## Overview

Use this skill to bring a repository to an AI-native operating baseline so humans and AI agents can work safely, quickly, and with enough context to make good decisions.

This skill is not a generic template copier. It classifies repository maturity, audits local and remote state, applies or proposes the right baseline, preserves intentional maturity-based exceptions, and verifies the result.

Prefer judgment over box-checking. File presence alone is not enough; audit whether the repository is truthful about its current state, whether commands are actually runnable, and whether any template residue or placeholder content still misleads users or agents.

When GitHub governance is in scope, keep three states separate in both reasoning and reporting:

- local desired state stored in repo files
- live remote state currently configured on GitHub
- default-branch activation state for workflows and protections that may lag local branch changes

<content>1: # Baseline Rubric
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: This rubric defines what good looks like for an AI-native repository baseline.
6: 
7: Use it to audit a repository, decide what to add or upgrade, and explain why a repository is or is not ready for effective AI-assisted development.
8: 
9: Judge semantic truth, not just artifact presence. A repository with all expected files can still be misleading if commands are not runnable, comments still reflect an old template, or placeholders invalidate governance signals.
10: 
11: ## Cross-Cutting Audit Rules
12: 
13: ### Current-State Truthfulness
14: 
15: - verify that commands documented as current setup steps are supported by the repository contents
16: - separate planned stack declarations from currently runnable commands
17: - treat command-to-filesystem mismatch as a meaningful gap even when docs are otherwise strong
18: 
19: Evidence sources for command realism:
20: 
21: - package manifests such as `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, or `go.mod`
22: - lockfiles such as `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `package-lock.json`, or `uv.lock`
23: - workflow files that run the same commands in CI
24: - tool config files such as `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `eslint.config.*`, or `tsconfig.json`
25: - scripts declared in package-manager metadata or documented bootstrap scripts
26: 
27: Audit surfaces that need the same scrutiny:
28: 
29: - `README.md`
30: - `AGENTS.md`
31: - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
32: - development/setup docs
33: - workflow comments and release notes that describe current behavior
34: 
35: Heuristics for docs-first repos:
36: 
37: - if docs use present-tense verbs like `run`, `install`, `start`, or `test`, verify supporting manifests or toolchain files exist
38: - if a planned stack is named but no manifests or scripts exist, require wording that labels commands as future-state or illustrative
39: - prefer `pre-commit` or other provisioned entrypoints over standalone CLIs that are not clearly installed by the repo
40: - treat agent-only instructions as real repo guidance, not exempt examples; `AGENTS.md` should meet the same truthfulness bar as user-facing docs
41: 
42: ### Placeholder Taxonomy
43: 
44: Classify placeholders before scoring them:
45: 
46: - `downstream-customization`: intended for adopters of a template repo
47: - `maturity-based`: acceptable because the repo has not reached that implementation stage yet
48: - `stale-template-residue`: inherited comments, framework references, or wording that no longer matches repo reality
49: - `governance-invalidating`: placeholders that weaken ownership, review, navigation, or trust in current-repo guidance
50: 
51: Not all placeholders are equal. Do not treat all placeholders as failures, and do not treat all placeholders as harmless.
52: 
53: Severity guidance:
54: 
55: - `governance-invalidating` placeholders are at least `medium` severity by default and may be `high` when they break ownership, review policy, support links, or safety boundaries
56: - `stale-template-residue` is usually `low` or `medium` depending on how misleading it is
57: - `maturity-based` and `downstream-customization` placeholders are acceptable when clearly labeled
58: 
59: Examples of governance-invalidating findings:
60: 
61: - `CODEOWNERS` entries that are placeholder-only or unlikely to resolve to real users or teams
62: - `CODEOWNERS` entries that use only an org handle such as `@org` instead of a user or `@org/team`
63: - issue-template or support links that point to broken placeholder URLs
64: - docs or agent instructions that present direct remote apply commands without audit-first or approval-gated framing
65: - docs that normalize `--no-verify` or similar hook bypasses as standard workflow guidance
66: 
67: ### Semantic Equivalence Over Exact Paths
68: 
69: Prefer meaning over exact casing or path conventions when the repo intent is clear. For example, `docs/development.md` and `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` may satisfy the same role.
70: 
71: ## Category 1: Repo Intent Is Legible
72: 
73: ### Required Signals
74: 
75: - `README.md` explains what the repo is, what state it is in, and how to get started.
76: - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` captures implemented or target architecture.
77: - `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` provides local setup and quality commands.
78: - `AGENTS.md` gives repo-specific instructions to AI agents.
79: 
80: ### Good Looks Like
81: 
82: - minimal ambiguity about repo purpose and maturity
83: - no unresolved identity placeholders in the primary entry docs
84: - enough context for an AI agent to infer guardrails without guessing
85: - current local commands are clearly distinguishable from future-state plans
86: - stale framework or product references are absent
87: 
88: ## Category 2: Governance Is Standardized Early
89: 
90: ### Required Signals
91: 
92: - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
93: - `.github/pull_request_template.md`
94: - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*`
95: - `.github/CODEOWNERS`
96: 
97: ### Good Looks Like
98: 
99: - PRs explain why, what changed, and how work was validated
100: - issue templates collect actionable information
101: - ownership is concrete rather than generic placeholder-only content
102: - contribution docs match real repo gates and workflow
103: - CODEOWNERS intent is clear about whether it is advisory or enforced
104: - CODEOWNERS entries look syntactically valid for GitHub user or team resolution
105: - hook bypasses are absent from normal workflow guidance or are scoped as rare human-only exceptions
106: 
107: ## Category 3: Local Feedback Loops Exist
108: 
109: ### Required Signals
110: 
111: - `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
112: - `.markdownlint.yaml`
113: - `.gitignore`
114: 
115: ### Baseline Checks
116: 
117: - YAML validation
118: - TOML validation where relevant
119: - end-of-file fixes
120: - trailing whitespace cleanup
121: - large-file checks
122: - markdown lint and fix
123: - Conventional Commit enforcement
124: - secret scanning
125: - Renovate config validation when Renovate is present
126: 
127: ### Good Looks Like
128: 
129: - one reliable baseline verification command such as `pre-commit run --all-files`
130: - tech-agnostic checks land before language-specific hooks
131: - language-specific hooks are added when implementation maturity justifies them
132: - docs and agent instructions prefer provisioned commands over assumed globally installed tools
133: 
134: ## Category 4: CI Preserves Quality Continuously
135: 
136: ### Required Signals
137: 
138: - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
139: 
140: ### Good Looks Like
141: 
142: - required status checks exist even in docs-first repositories
143: - CI mirrors local quality expectations rather than diverging from them
144: - maturity-aware jobs are used instead of fake implementation claims
145: - placeholder test jobs are transparent about why they exist
146: 
147: ### Docs-First Acceptable Pattern
148: 
149: - a placeholder or documentation-only test gate may exist to preserve required checks while implementation is still pending
150: 
151: ## Category 5: Release Path Is Pre-Wired
152: 
153: ### Required Signals
154: 
155: - `.github/workflows/release.yml`
156: - `.releaserc.toml`
157: 
158: ### Good Looks Like
159: 
160: - release automation is scaffolded early but configured honestly
161: - Conventional Commits are the release source of truth
162: - docs-first repositories may leave stamp targets empty if no versioned artifacts exist yet
163: - release docs do not imply artifact stamping or packaging that the repo does not actually perform yet
164: 
165: ## Category 6: Remote GitHub Policy Is Treated As Code
166: 
167: ### Required Signals
168: 
169: - `.github/ruleset-config.json`
170: 
171: ### Minimum Acceptable State
172: 
173: - desired branch governance is versioned in the repo
174: - repo-level settings are at least auditable and documented if not machine-readable yet
175: - local desired state can be compared to live remote state without guessing the intended policy
176: - repo-settings desired state appears as a first-class audit output, not buried in prose examples
177: 
178: ### Stronger Signals
179: 
180: - a remote ruleset sync workflow
181: - CI drift detection against live GitHub ruleset state
182: - documented repo settings audit or apply commands
183: - repo-settings desired state captured in a table or machine-readable definition
184: 
185: ### Good Looks Like
186: 
187: - desired branch governance is versioned in the repo
188: - required checks and merge policy are explicit
189: - live GitHub state can be inspected and reconciled from the repo definition
190: - repo-level settings such as merge methods or delete-branch-on-merge are not left as pure tribal knowledge
191: - reports distinguish local desired state from live remote state and from default-branch activation status
192: 
193: ## Category 7: AI Operability Is Explicit
194: 
195: ### Required Signals
196: 
197: - `AGENTS.md`
198: 
199: ### Optional Signals
200: 
201: - `.claude/`
202: - `.cursor/`
203: - `.opencode/`
204: - other agent runtime config files that are actually used by the team
205: 
206: ### Good Looks Like
207: 
208: - repo-specific AI instructions
209: - concise quality commands
210: - explicit constraints, not generic boilerplate
211: - clear notes on placeholders, maturity, and safety expectations
212: - commands named in AI instructions are likely runnable in a fresh repo environment
213: - remote mutation guidance is clearly separated from audit steps and approval boundaries
214: 
215: ## Category 8: Environment And Integration Surfaces Are Documented
216: 
217: ### Conditional Signals
218: 
219: - `.env.example`
220: - repo docs describing required integrations
221: 
222: ### Good Looks Like
223: 
224: - placeholders only, never real secrets
225: - enough detail to understand planned external surfaces
226: - future integrations may be documented before implementation is complete
227: - variables can be annotated by phase when some are not immediately needed
228: 
229: ## Category 9: Maturity-Based Exceptions Are Intentional
230: 
231: ### Acceptable Docs-First Exceptions
232: 
233: - placeholder test job to preserve required checks
234: - empty semantic-release stamp targets
235: - commented language-specific hooks
236: - target-state architecture documentation instead of implementation-detail docs
237: - template-repo placeholders that are clearly marked as downstream customization points
238: 
239: ### Not Acceptable
240: 
241: - broken or contradictory placeholders
242: - stale template residue that points to the wrong framework, product, or workflow
243: - missing required review or status-check contracts
244: - tribal-knowledge-only remote settings
245: - generic template text that no longer matches repo reality
246: 
247: ## Minimum Acceptable State
248: 
249: A repository is minimally ready when it has:
250: 
251: - legible repo-purpose docs
252: - truthful current-state commands
253: - repo-specific governance docs
254: - runnable local baseline checks
255: - CI enforcing the baseline
256: - release scaffolding
257: - ruleset-as-code or equivalent governance intent captured in repo
258: - AI instructions in `AGENTS.md`
259: 
260: ## Strong Target State
261: 
262: A repository is strongly AI-native when it also has:
263: 
264: - remote GitHub parity audit or sync guidance
265: - ruleset drift detection
266: - maturity-aware exceptions documented clearly
267: - placeholder types explicitly separated and justified
268: - repo-specific environment and integration surfaces documented
269: - little to no gap between declared and live governance
270: - little to no stale template residue

(End of file - total 270 lines)
</content>

<type>file</type>
<content>1: # Maturity Profiles
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: Use these profiles to classify the target repository before recommending baseline changes.
6: 
7: The profile determines what is required now, what is recommended next, and what should be explicitly deferred.
8: 
9: Use the lowest profile that honestly fits the repository today. Advanced governance or release scaffolding should not inflate maturity if implementation is still absent.
10: 
11: ## Template-Repo Lens
12: 
13: Some repositories are themselves template products. They may be `docs-first` in implementation while still being mature in repository operations such as release automation, contribution scaffolding, or governance-as-code.
14: 
15: When auditing a template repo:
16: 
17: - separate current-repo truth from downstream customization guidance
18: - treat downstream placeholders differently from misleading current-repo placeholders
19: - do not require application-specific runtime surfaces if the template has no implementation yet
20: 
21: Examples:
22: 
23: - acceptable: a downstream `README` snippet that says "replace `{OWNER}/{REPO}` when creating your project"
24: - not acceptable: the live template repo's own `CODEOWNERS` or support links still using unresolved placeholders
25: - acceptable: a target-state architecture document clearly labeled for adopters
26: - not acceptable: present-tense setup commands for tooling the template repo does not actually provision
27: 
28: ## Profile: Docs-First
29: 
30: ### Description
31: 
32: The repository primarily contains planning, governance, architecture, or product documentation. Application code is absent or intentionally minimal.
33: 
34: ### Typical Signals
35: 
36: - strong `README.md`, `PRD`, or architecture docs
37: - little or no application code
38: - no real test suite yet
39: - CI focused on docs hygiene, policy drift, and baseline hooks
40: 
41: ### Required Baseline
42: 
43: - repo-specific docs and governance files
44: - `pre-commit` baseline
45: - CI with required status checks preserved
46: - release scaffolding with honest defaults
47: - ruleset-as-code
48: - `AGENTS.md`
49: 
50: ### Acceptable Exceptions
51: 
52: - placeholder test gate in CI
53: - empty release stamping targets
54: - commented language-specific hooks
55: - target-state architecture rather than implementation detail
56: - template scaffolding that is clearly presented as downstream customization guidance
57: 
58: ### Common Mistakes
59: 
60: - pretending tests or builds exist when they do not
61: - leaving template placeholders unresolved
62: - mixing future-state commands with current-state setup guidance
63: - deferring branch governance until later
64: - omitting AI instructions because the repo has no code yet
65: 
66: ## Profile: Early Implementation
67: 
68: ### Description
69: 
70: The repository contains real implementation work, but the delivery system and contributor workflow are still maturing.
71: 
72: ### Typical Signals
73: 
74: - application code exists
75: - at least one real lint or test command exists
76: - documentation and governance may still be partly template-derived
77: - release configuration may be scaffolded but incomplete
78: 
79: ### Required Baseline
80: 
81: - everything in `docs-first`
82: - real CI lint and test commands where available
83: - initial language-specific hooks where they add value
84: - environment examples for implemented integrations
85: 
86: ### Acceptable Exceptions
87: 
88: - partial release stamping if the packaging story is still forming
89: - limited integration documentation when systems are still being introduced
90: 
91: ### Common Mistakes
92: 
93: - keeping placeholder CI while code already exists
94: - relying on tribal knowledge for environment setup
95: - adding code without updating `AGENTS.md` or `CONTRIBUTING.md`
96: - leaving stale template comments that contradict the implemented stack
97: 
98: ## Profile: Active Delivery
99: 
100: ### Description
101: 
102: The repository is actively delivering application or platform changes with established team workflows and real automation expectations.
103: 
104: ### Typical Signals
105: 
106: - meaningful application code and tests
107: - release expectations are real, not just scaffolded
108: - dependency management and CI are part of the daily workflow
109: - reviewers depend on explicit governance and ownership
110: 
111: ### Required Baseline
112: 
113: - everything in `early-implementation`
114: - real and enforced required status checks
115: - coherent release stamping and artifacts
116: - ruleset parity or equivalent remote governance verification
117: - environment and integration docs that reflect production reality
118: 
119: ### Acceptable Exceptions
120: 
121: - only repo-specific, explicitly documented deviations justified by architecture, compliance, or team workflow
122: 
123: ### Common Mistakes
124: 
125: - release automation that exists but is not trustworthy
126: - CI drifting away from local workflows
127: - remote governance that no longer matches repo policy files
128: 
129: ## Escalation Rules
130: 
131: - If the repository looks docs-first but remote governance is missing, treat that as a real gap, not a harmless deferral.
132: - If code exists but CI still uses placeholders, escalate to `early-implementation` remediation.
133: - If release or ruleset state is live but undocumented, escalate remediation, but do not automatically reclassify the repository to `active-delivery`.
134: 
135: ## Decision Checks
136: 
137: Before finalizing the profile, ask:
138: 
139: - Are the documented commands actually runnable today?
140: - Is this repo shipping a product, or is the repo itself the product scaffold?
141: - Are advanced governance patterns real operational needs, or just prepared scaffolding?
142: - Are exceptions intentional and transparent, or are they unresolved contradictions?
143: 
144: ## Decision Rule
145: 
146: Choose the lowest profile that honestly fits the repository today, then use that profile to avoid overscoping the bootstrap work.

(End of file - total 146 lines)
</content>

<content>1: # AI Prompts and Skills
2: 
3: A collection of reusable prompts and skills for AI tools used in day-to-day software development.
4: 
5: [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-Apache_2.0-blue.svg)](https://github.com/liatrio-labs/ai-prompts/blob/main/LICENSE)
6: 
7: ## About This Collection
8: 
9: This collection contains curated prompts and skills for AI tools that support day-to-day software development activities. Prompts and skills are organized by use case and designed to be integrated into different assistant workflows.
10: 
11: ## Quick Start
12: 
13: ### 1. Clone the Repository
14: 
15: ```bash
16: git clone https://github.com/liatrio-labs/ai-prompts.git
17: cd ai-prompts
18: ```
19: 
20: ### 2. Browse Prompts and Skills
21: 
22: Prompts are organized in the `prompts/` directory by category:
23: 
24: - `development/` - Development workflow and coding prompts
25: - `documentation-and-research/` - Documentation, content generation, and research prompts
26: 
27: Skills are organized in the `skills/` directory by capability:
28: 
29: - `git-commit-conventional/` - Conventional commit message and commit hygiene guidance
30: - `create-pull-request/` - PR/MR drafting and optional creation workflow
31: - `create-mermaid-diagrams/` - Mermaid diagram creation and validation workflow
32: - `liatrio-brand-guidelines/` - Liatrio visual brand standards and UI compliance guidance
33: - `mastra-api/` - Mastra development API debugging and inspection workflows
34: - `tilt-dev/` - Tilt-based local development environment operations
35: - `uv-usage/` - `uv` package manager workflows and command guidance
36: - `branch-surgery-pr-split/` - Branch decomposition strategy for splitting oversized or mixed-concern PRs
37: - `ai-native-repo-bootstrap/` - Repository bootstrap and upgrade workflow for AI-native development guardrails
38: 
39: ### 3. Use Prompts with AI Tools
40: 
41: **Option A: Direct Copy**
42: Copy prompts directly from the files into your preferred AI tool (Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, etc.) and customize the parameters as needed for your specific use case.
43: 
44: **Option B: Install with Slash Command Manager (Recommended)**
45: Use the [Slash Command Manager](https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager) to automatically install prompts for your AI coding assistant. The tool will detect your installed AI assistants and let you choose where to install:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: # Install development prompts
49: uvx --from git+https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager slash-man generate \
50:   --github-repo liatrio-labs/ai-prompts \
51:   --github-branch main \
52:   --github-path prompts/development
53: 
54: # Install documentation and research prompts
55: uvx --from git+https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager slash-man generate \
56:   --github-repo liatrio-labs/ai-prompts \
57:   --github-branch main \
58:   --github-path prompts/documentation-and-research
59: ```
60: 
61: See the [Slash Command Manager documentation](https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager) for more details.
62: 
63: ### 4. Use Skills with `npx skills`
64: 
65: Install repository skills using the [`skills.sh`](https://skills.sh) CLI:
66: 
67: ```bash
68: # List available skills in this repository without installing
69: npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --list
70: 
71: # Add all skills from this repository to your local setup (non-interactive)
72: npx -y skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --all
73: 
74: # Install all skills from this repository
75: npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --all
76: 
77: # Install specific skills
78: npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --skill git-commit-conventional --skill create-pull-request
79: 
80: # Install to specific agents
81: npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --skill create-mermaid-diagrams -a cursor -a codex
82: 
83: # Install globally instead of project scope
84: npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --skill git-commit-conventional -g
85: ```
86: 
87: Compatibility notes:
88: 
89: - `allowed-tools` support varies by agent, so keep frontmatter portable across assistants.
90: - Use `DISABLE_TELEMETRY=1` (or `DO_NOT_TRACK=1`) to opt out of telemetry when running the CLI.
91: - If internal skills are introduced later, prefix the command with `INSTALL_INTERNAL_SKILLS=1` (for example, `INSTALL_INTERNAL_SKILLS=1 npx skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --list`) to include them in discovery.
92: 
93: ## Skill Creation Scripts (Agent-Focused)
94: 
95: This repository includes local helper scripts for AI-agent-driven skill authoring workflows.
96: These scripts are derived from OpenAI's `skills/.system/skill-creator` in `openai/skills`: https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.system/skill-creator
97: 
98: ```bash

103: uv run scripts/init_skill.py my-skill --path skills --resources scripts,references --examples
104: 
105: # Generate or refresh agents/openai.yaml
106: uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/my-skill
107: ```
108: 
109: For a fuller workflow and troubleshooting notes, see [docs/development.md](docs/development.md).
110: 
111: ## Context Markers Quick Reference
112: 
113: Context Markers are emoji-based verification techniques used to detect context rot and ensure critical instructions are being processed correctly by AI agents. This approach provides immediate visual feedback that instructions are being followed, addressing the systematic degradation of AI performance as context length increases. The technique involves requiring AI responses to begin with specific emoji markers, giving users a coarse check that may help detect when context instructions have been lost due to context window limitations or compaction inefficiencies.

43: 
44: ### Pull Request Process
45: 
46: 1. Ensure your PR description clearly describes the problem and solution
47: 2. Link any related issues
48: 3. Ensure all CI checks pass
49: 4. Request review from maintainers
50: 5. Address feedback promptly
51: 
52: ## Conventional Commits
53: 
54: We follow the [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) specification for all commit messages. This enables automated versioning and changelog generation.
55: 
56: ### Format
57: 
58: ```text
59: <type>[optional scope]: <description>
60: 
61: [optional body]
62: 
63: [optional footer(s)]
64: ```
65: 
66: ### Types
67: 
68: - **feat**: A new feature (triggers minor version bump)
69: - **fix**: A bug fix (triggers patch version bump)
70: - **docs**: Documentation only changes
71: - **style**: Changes that don't affect code meaning (formatting, whitespace)
72: - **refactor**: Code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature
73: - **perf**: Performance improvement (triggers patch version bump)
74: - **test**: Adding or updating tests
75: - **build**: Changes to build system or dependencies
76: - **ci**: Changes to CI configuration files and scripts
77: - **chore**: Other changes that don't modify src or test files
78: - **revert**: Reverts a previous commit
79: 
80: ### Examples
81: 
82: ```bash
83: # Feature with description
84: git commit -m "feat: add user authentication"
85: 
86: # Bug fix with scope
87: git commit -m "fix(api): handle null response from external service"
88: 
89: # Breaking change (triggers major version bump)
90: git commit -m "feat!: redesign API authentication" -m "BREAKING CHANGE: old API keys no longer supported"
91: 
92: # Multiple commit message lines
93: git commit -m "feat: add data export functionality" -m "- Export to CSV format" -m "- Export to JSON format"
94: ```
95: 
96: ### Scope (Optional)
97: 
98: Scope provides additional context about what part of the codebase changed:
99: 
100: - `feat(auth): add OAuth2 support`
101: - `fix(database): resolve connection pool leak`
102: - `docs(readme): update installation instructions`
103: 
104: ### Breaking Changes
105: 
106: For breaking changes, add `!` after the type/scope or include `BREAKING CHANGE:` in the footer:
107: 
108: ```bash
109: git commit -m "feat!: remove deprecated API endpoints"
110: 
111: # OR
112: 
113: git commit -m "feat: redesign authentication system" -m "BREAKING CHANGE: all existing tokens are invalidated"
114: ```
115: 
116: ## Branch Naming Conventions
117: 
118: Use descriptive branch names that indicate the type of work:
119: 
120: ### Format
121: 
122: ```text
123: <type>/<short-description>
124: ```
125: 
126: ### Examples
127: 
128: - `feat/user-authentication`
129: - `fix/database-connection-leak`
130: - `docs/update-readme`
131: - `refactor/simplify-api-handlers`
132: - `test/add-integration-tests`
133: - `chore/update-dependencies`
134: 
135: ### Guidelines
136: 
137: - Use lowercase with hyphens (kebab-case)
138: - Keep names concise but descriptive
139: - Use the same type prefixes as commits (feat, fix, docs, etc.)
140: 
141: ## Quality Gates
142: 
143: Run these checks before opening or updating a PR:
144: 
145: ```bash
146: pre-commit run --all-files
147: python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
148: ```
149: 
150: Current pre-commit hooks in this repository cover:

3: This repository stores reusable AI prompts and skills. Most automation revolves around Markdown quality gates and release workflows, so local setup is intentionally lightweight.

6: 
7: ### Prerequisites
8:

11: - `pipx` or `pip` for installing Python CLIs
12: - [`uv`](https://github.com/astral-sh/uv) for skill authoring helper scripts and optional Slash Command Manager workflows
13:

15: 
16: ```bash
17: # Clone the repository
18: git clone https://github.com/liatrio-labs/ai-prompts.git
19: cd ai-prompts
20: 
21: # Install pre-commit and hooks
22: pip install --user pre-commit
23: pre-commit install
24: ```
25: 
26: ### Running Quality Gates Locally
27: 
28: ```bash
29: # Run every hook (YAML, Markdown lint, commitlint config, gitleaks)
30: pre-commit run --all-files
31: 
32: # Spot-check a single file while editing
33: pre-commit run markdownlint-fix --files prompts/development/address-pr-review-feedback.md
34: ```
35: 
36: ## Skill Authoring Helpers
37: 
38: These local scripts support creating and maintaining skills in this repository, primarily for AI-agent workflows.
39: 
40: | Script | Purpose | Common Command |
41: | --- | --- | --- |
42: | `scripts/init_skill.py` | Create a new skill scaffold with optional resource folders/examples | `uv run scripts/init_skill.py my-skill --path skills --resources scripts,references --examples` |
43: | `scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py` | Create or refresh `agents/openai.yaml` from skill metadata | `uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/my-skill` |
44: | `scripts/quick_validate.py` | Validate skill frontmatter and naming conventions | `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/my-skill` |
45: 
46: ### Recommended Workflow
47: 
48: 1. Create a new skill scaffold.
49: 2. Complete `SKILL.md` frontmatter and body, then add resources.
50: 3. Generate or refresh `agents/openai.yaml`.
51: 4. Run quick validation.
52: 
53: ```bash
54: uv run scripts/init_skill.py my-skill --path skills --resources scripts,references --examples
55: uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/my-skill
56: uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/my-skill
57: ```
58: 
59: ### Troubleshooting Skill Scripts
60: 
61: If `quick_validate.py` reports `Description must be a string, got list`, ensure `description:` in `SKILL.md` frontmatter is a quoted YAML string.
62: 
63: ### Skills.sh Compatibility Checklist
64: 
65: Use this checklist before publishing or recommending a skill for `npx skills` consumption:
66: 
67: 1. `SKILL.md` exists in `skills/<skill-name>/`.
68: 2. Frontmatter includes non-empty `name` and `description` values.
69: 3. `name` is hyphen-case (lowercase letters, digits, hyphens only) and matches directory naming.
70: 4. Optional frontmatter keys remain portable across agents (for example, use `allowed-tools` only when needed).
71: 5. `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/<skill-name>` passes.
72: 
73: ### Documentation Drift Check
74: 
75: Run this check whenever you add/remove skills or update repository documentation sections that reference skills or scripts:
76: 
77: ```bash
78: python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
79: ```
80: 
81: ### Adding or Editing Prompts and Skills
82: 
83: 1. For prompts, create/update files inside `prompts/<category>/` using Markdown.
84: 2. For skills, scaffold/update under `skills/<skill-name>/` and include `SKILL.md`.
85: 3. Follow existing structure and conventions for the selected artifact type.
86: 4. Run `pre-commit run --all-files` to ensure formatting/linting passes.
87: 5. Open a PR using the template checklist (ensures docs + CI expectations are met).
88: 
89: ## Slash Command Manager (Optional)
90: 
91: Quickly install prompts into an AI assistant. The tool will detect your installed AI assistants and prompt you to choose:
92: 
93: ```bash
94: # Install development prompts
95: uvx --from git+https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager slash-man generate \
96:   --github-repo liatrio-labs/ai-prompts \
97:   --github-branch main \
98:   --github-path prompts/development
99: 
100: # Install documentation prompts
101: uvx --from git+https://github.com/liatrio-labs/slash-command-manager slash-man generate \
102:   --github-repo liatrio-labs/ai-prompts \
103:   --github-branch main \
104:   --github-path prompts/documentation-and-research
105: ```
106: 
107: ## Release Workflow Notes
108: 
109: Semantic Release runs after the CI workflow succeeds on `main`. To publish successfully:
110: 
111: - Ensure `.github/workflows/ci.yml` passes on the PR
112: - Use conventional commit messages so release automation can derive version bumps
113: 
114: ## Troubleshooting
115: 
116: | Issue | Fix |
117: | --- | --- |
118: | `pre-commit` is missing | Re-run `pip install pre-commit && pre-commit install` |
119: | Markdown lint fails on tables/lists | Use `markdownlint --fix <file>` or let the hook auto-fix |
120: | `quick_validate.py` reports a frontmatter type error | Follow the `Troubleshooting Skill Scripts` section above and ensure frontmatter values use valid YAML scalar strings |
121: | Release workflow cannot mint a token | Verify the Chainguard STS subject matches `liatrio-labs/ai-prompts` and rerun the job |
122: | Branch protection blocks merge | Confirm all required checks have completed on the PR and re-run failed jobs from GitHub Actions if needed |

+# Local-Only Bootstrap Audit
+
+## Repository Summary
+
+- Repository path: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/ai-prompts`
+- Repository purpose: reusable prompts and skills for AI-assisted software development, documented in `README.md`
+- Current maturity profile: `docs-first`
+- Template-repo lens needed: partial; the repo ships reusable AI assets, but several files still carry generic template wording that should be localized
+- Workflow used: `upgrade_repo`
+
+## Scope Boundary
+
+- This audit covers only local docs, `AGENTS.md`, local guardrails, CI and release honesty, and verification steps.
+- Live GitHub remote settings, rulesets, and default-branch activation were intentionally not inspected or discussed.
+
+## Compliant Baseline Items
+
+- `README.md` makes repo purpose, contents, and contributor entry points legible.
+- `AGENTS.md` is repo-specific and points agents to the local `uv run` skill workflow plus the docs-drift guardrail.
+- `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `.github/pull_request_template.md`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*`, and `.github/CODEOWNERS` are present.
+- `.pre-commit-config.yaml` provides tech-agnostic local guardrails: YAML/TOML checks, file hygiene, Markdown linting, secret scanning, docs-drift validation, and Conventional Commit enforcement on `commit-msg`.
+- `docs/development.md` documents local setup and skill-helper scripts, and the referenced scripts exist under `scripts/`.
+- Local verification evidence is strong: `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`, `pre-commit run --all-files`, and `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap` all passed during this audit.
+
+## Truthfulness And Command Realism
+
+- Current runnable commands are well supported for the docs-first workflow: `pre-commit run --all-files`, `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`, and the `uv run scripts/*.py` helper commands are backed by real files and inline script metadata.
+- `scripts/init_skill.py`, `scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py`, and `scripts/quick_validate.py` use `uv` script metadata rather than a repo-level Python project file, so the documented `uv run ...` pattern is locally plausible and maturity-appropriate.
+- The main truthfulness gap is test language: `CONTRIBUTING.md` says contributors should "Ensure all tests pass," `.github/pull_request_template.md` asks authors to confirm tests passed, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml` is named `CI - Tests and Linting`, but no test suite or test config is present in the repository.
+- `pre-commit run --all-files` is a real baseline verification command, but it does not exercise the `commit-msg` hook. Current docs should say that content checks run via `pre-commit`, while Conventional Commit validation happens when creating a commit.
+
+## Placeholder And Template Residue Findings
+
+- Downstream-customization placeholders: language-specific examples in `.releaserc.toml` remain acceptable as optional scaffolding when clearly framed as examples.
+- Maturity-based placeholders: empty `version_variables` and `assets` in `.releaserc.toml` are honest for a docs-first repo that does not yet publish packaged artifacts.
+- Stale template residue: `.github/workflows/release.yml` and `.releaserc.toml` still include "this template" and generic project-customization commentary that does not fully match this repository's current identity.
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders: `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` links users to `open-source-project-template` docs instead of this repository's own `README.md` and `docs/development.md`.
+
+## Gaps By Severity
+
+### High
+
+- CI honesty is misaligned across `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `.github/pull_request_template.md`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml`: the repo currently enforces local guardrails, not tests, so the wording overclaims current delivery maturity.
+
+### Medium
+
+- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` contains broken-for-this-repo navigation links, which is a governance-invalidating placeholder and should be corrected early.
+- `AGENTS.md` lacks an explicit local verification section that names the repository's core guardrail commands, so agents must infer verification steps from other docs.
+- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` is missing, leaving no single top-level explanation of repository structure, intended operating model, and docs-first boundaries.
+- Release scaffolding is directionally correct for a docs-first repo, but stale generic-template comments in `.github/workflows/release.yml` and `.releaserc.toml` weaken current-state truthfulness.
+- Verification guidance does not clearly separate all-files validation from commit-message validation, which can confuse contributors about what has and has not been checked locally.
+
+### Low
+
+- The repo would benefit from one short, repeated local bootstrap checklist shared across `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `docs/development.md`, and `AGENTS.md` so humans and agents follow the same maturity-aware path.
+
+## Minimum Baseline Vs Stronger Target State
+
+- Minimum acceptable baseline gaps: fix the test-language mismatch, correct issue-template contact links, add a top-level architecture/operating-model doc, and add explicit verification guidance to `AGENTS.md`.
+- Stronger target-state improvements: add a concise docs-first CI note that explains why there is no app test job yet, and centralize a single local verification checklist that all contributor-facing docs reuse.
+
+## Governance Placeholder Severity
+
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders: wrong contact links in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`.
+- Advisory placeholders that are acceptable for now: empty release stamp targets in `.releaserc.toml` and example-only language-specific release notes.
+- CODEOWNERS validity or enforcement notes: `.github/CODEOWNERS` uses a concrete team handle and is locally plausible; live resolution and enforcement were intentionally not audited.
+- Unsafe direct-apply or hook-bypass guidance: none found in the audited local docs; `CONTRIBUTING.md` explicitly says not to bypass hooks.
+
+## Local Desired State Summary
+
+- Core docs should describe the repository as a docs-first prompts-and-skills repo with real local guardrails and no assumed application test suite.
+- `AGENTS.md` should carry a short "verify locally" section with `pre-commit run --all-files`, `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`, and the relevant `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py ...` pattern for skill work.
+- CI naming and PR checklist language should match the current baseline honestly, for example by talking about guardrails, docs checks, and hooks rather than nonexistent tests.
+- Release scaffolding should stay pre-wired, but comments should be rewritten to describe this repo rather than a generic template consumer workflow.
+- A top-level `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` should explain repository layout, skill-resource conventions, and why docs-first exceptions are intentional.
+
+## Local Remediation Plan
+
+- Planned changes:
+  - Rewrite test-related wording in `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `.github/pull_request_template.md`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml` so it reflects the real checks that exist today.
+  - Fix `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` to point at this repo's local docs.
+  - Add a concise verification section to `AGENTS.md`.
+  - Add `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` with a maturity-aware operating model for prompts, skills, helper scripts, and agent config directories.
+  - Remove or localize stale template commentary in `.github/workflows/release.yml` and `.releaserc.toml`.
+  - Clarify in contributor docs that commit-message linting is enforced at commit time, not by `pre-commit run --all-files`.
+- Applied changes: none; this was planning only.
+- Deferred local items: any remote-governance or live-GitHub parity work remains intentionally out of scope for this eval.
+
+## Validation Results
+
+- Commands run:
+  - `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`
+  - `pre-commit run --all-files`
+  - `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`
+- Passing checks:
+  - Documentation drift check passed.
+  - Pre-commit baseline hooks passed.
+  - Skill validation passed for `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+- Failing or skipped checks:
+  - No failing local checks were observed in the commands run.
+  - Application test verification was skipped because no application test suite is currently present.
+  - Live remote governance verification was skipped by explicit prompt constraint.
+
+## Residual Risks And Follow-Ups
+
+- Until the wording is corrected, contributors and AI agents can over-assume that a real test suite exists and is enforced.
+- Wrong issue-template contact links reduce trust in governance docs and route users to unrelated documentation.
+- Without a top-level architecture or operating-model doc, new agents may overgeneralize from individual skill folders instead of the repo's actual docs-first workflow.
+- The repository is close to a solid docs-first AI-native baseline locally; the remaining work is mostly about making existing guardrails more truthful, more centralized, and easier for both humans and agents to follow.
*** Add File: skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap-workspace/iteration-1/local-only-bootstrap/old_skill/outputs/user_notes.md
+# Notes
+
+- Repo classification: `docs-first`; do not assume app code or a real test suite already exists.
+- Commands verified during this audit all passed: `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`, `pre-commit run --all-files`, and `uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+- Highest-priority local gap: docs and CI language still claim or imply tests that the repo does not currently have.
+- Other important local gaps: wrong issue-template contact links, missing top-level `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and no explicit verification section in `AGENTS.md`.
+- Remote GitHub settings were intentionally not inspected or discussed for this eval.
*** End Patch
